title: CSS Foundations
tagline: Make things colorful, sized, and positioned.

accomplishments: Change the background color of a webpage

do.title: Make your webpage turn blue
steps: Open VS Code (or use CodePen.io if you don't have VS Code installed yet)
Create a new file and save it as colors.html
Copy and paste the code below
Double-click the file to open it in your browser
Your whole page should have a light blue background!
do.code:
Look! A blue background!

This is my first styled webpage.
do.result: You just used CSS to change the background color of your entire webpage!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing your light blue webpage.

concepts.name: The <style> tag
concepts.definition: This is where CSS code lives inside an HTML file. Everything between <style> and </style> is CSS, not HTML.
concepts.analogy: A painter's instructions before they start painting. The <style> tag says "Here's how I want things to look."

concepts.name: The body selector
concepts.definition: This tells CSS "I want to style the body element." The body is the entire visible part of your webpage — everything you can see.
concepts.analogy: Pointing at a room and saying "paint THIS room blue." The selector picks what to paint.

concepts.name: The background-color property
concepts.definition: This is the instruction that actually changes the color. You write the property name, then a colon, then the color you want, then a semicolon.
concepts.analogy: A recipe instruction: ingredient (selector), cooking method (property), specific measurement (value). body { background-color: lightblue; }

understand.keyTakeaway: CSS changes how things LOOK. HTML creates the things, CSS makes them pretty.

build.goal: Make the heading red while keeping the blue background

codeComparison.new:
<style>
  body {
    background-color: lightblue;
  }
  
  h1 {
    color: red;
  }
</style>

build.hint: Make sure you have both closing curly braces }. One closes the body rule, one closes the h1 rule.

challenge.title: Create a Traffic Light
challenge.mission: Create three headings that look like a traffic light — one red, one yellow, one green.
requirements: "Slow" is yellow (or gold — yellow is hard to read!)
challenge.starterCode:
Stop

Slow

Go
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add font-size: 60px; to make them HUGE like real traffic lights!

capabilities: Add CSS to an HTML file using the <style> tag
Target specific HTML elements with CSS selectors
Use classes to style different elements differently

bridge.preview: In Lesson 1.2, you'll learn to control text size, weight, and font families. You'll make headlines that actually look like headlines!

accomplishments: Make text bold without using <b> tags
Change the font to something that doesn't look like Times New Roman

do.title: Make a giant headline
steps: Create a new file and save it as text-styling.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in your browser
Your heading should be MASSIVE compared to the paragraph!
do.code:
I'M HUGE!

This is regular text for comparison.
do.result: You just used the font-size property to make text way bigger than default!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the size difference between your heading and paragraph.

concepts.name: The font-size property
concepts.definition: Controls how big or small your text appears. You can use px (pixels) for exact size, em for relative to parent, or rem for relative to root.
concepts.analogy: Setting the font size on a Word document, except you have way more control.

concepts.name: Pixels (px)
concepts.definition: The most common unit for beginners. 16px is default browser text. 72px is huge heading text. 12px is tiny fine print.
concepts.analogy: Like measuring in inches — exact, predictable, easy to understand.

concepts.name: font-weight
concepts.definition: Controls how thick or thin text appears. Values: normal (default), bold, or numbers like 100 (thin) to 900 (extra bold).
concepts.analogy: Like pressing harder or lighter with a pen — same letter, different thickness.

concepts.name: font-family
concepts.definition: Changes which font is used. Always provide a fallback: font-family: Arial, sans-serif means "use Arial, but if not available, use any sans-serif font."
concepts.analogy: Like choosing handwriting style — same words, completely different personality.

understand.keyTakeaway: font-size controls how big, font-weight controls how bold, font-family controls which font. Together they define your text's personality.

build.goal: Create a visual hierarchy with different text sizes — heading, subheading, and body text
build.hint: Each CSS property needs a semicolon ; at the end. If something doesn't work, check for missing semicolons!

challenge.title: Style a Blog Post Header
challenge.mission: Create a styled blog post header that looks like a real article.
requirements: Blog title in huge text (60px or bigger)
challenge.starterCode:
My First Blog Post

By [Your Name]

April 11, 2026

This is where the blog post content would go...
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a subtitle between the title and author using <h2>. Style it differently!

capabilities: Change text size with font-size and pixel values
Make text bold or light with font-weight
Create visual hierarchy with size differences

bridge.preview: In Lesson 1.3, you'll learn spacing — margins, padding, and borders. You'll finally understand why things are squished together and how to fix it!

accomplishments: Push elements away from each other with margins
Add breathing room inside boxes with padding
Understand the difference between margin and padding

do.title: Stop everything from touching the edges
steps: Create a new file and save it as spacing.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in your browser
See how the content is pushed away from the window edges!
do.code:
This heading has space around it now!

Notice how everything is pushed away from the edges?
do.result: You added margin to the body, which creates space between the edge of the browser window and your content!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the gray space around your content.

concepts.name: Margin
concepts.definition: Space OUTSIDE an element. It pushes other elements away. The background color does NOT fill the margin area.
concepts.analogy: The personal space bubble around a person. Margin is the "don't come closer than this" zone.

concepts.name: Padding
concepts.definition: Space INSIDE an element, between the border and the content. The background color DOES fill the padding area.
concepts.analogy: The cushioning inside a picture frame. The padding is between the frame edge and the photo itself.

concepts.name: Shorthand values
concepts.definition: One number applies to all sides: padding: 20px. Two numbers: top/bottom, left/right: padding: 15px 30px. Four numbers: top, right, bottom, left (clockwise).
concepts.analogy: Like giving directions — you can say "20 feet" (all sides) or "10 feet wide, 20 feet deep" (two values).

concepts.name: Margin vs Padding
concepts.definition: Margin = space outside (pushes things apart). Padding = space inside (gives content breathing room). Margin is transparent, padding shows the background color.
concepts.analogy: Margin is the yard around a house. Padding is the hallway inside the house between walls and furniture.

understand.keyTakeaway: Margin creates space OUTSIDE an element. Padding creates space INSIDE. Both are essential for layouts that don't look cramped.

build.goal: Add padding inside the heading and create a styled button with spacing
build.hint: padding: 15px 30px means 15px on top/bottom and 30px on left/right. This makes the button wider than it is tall.

challenge.title: Create a Card Layout
challenge.mission: Create a styled "card" that looks like a social media post or product card.
requirements: Card has a background color different from the page
Card has padding inside (so content isn't touching edges)
Card has margin around it (space from page edges)
Card contains a heading, paragraph, and button
challenge.starterCode:
Product Name

This is a description of the product.

Buy Now
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add border-radius: 8px; to your card to give it rounded corners!

capabilities: Add margin to create space OUTSIDE elements
Add padding to create space INSIDE elements
Understand the difference between margin and padding
Build card layouts with proper spacing

bridge.preview: In Lesson 1.4, you'll learn to add borders around elements, control their thickness and style, and understand the complete box model. You'll make actual boxes!
